verses.text: When his son falls sick, Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to the prophet Ahijah, hoping for a word. He wants God's prophet in trouble though he forsook God in prosperity. Men who ignore the LORD when all is well still run to Him when calamity strikes.

When his son falls sick, Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to the prophet Ahijah, hoping for a word. He wants God's prophet in trouble though he forsook God in prosperity. Men who ignore the LORD when all is well still run to Him when calamity strikes.

When his son falls sick, Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to the prophet Ahijah, hoping for a word. He wants God's prophet in trouble though he forsook God in prosperity. Men who ignore the LORD when all is well still run to Him when calamity strikes.

Ahijah is blind with age, but God tells him all, so he greets her before she speaks: 'why feignest thou thyself to be another?' No disguise fools the God who sees the heart. We may hide our face from men, but never our true self from the Lord.

Ahijah is blind with age, but God tells him all, so he greets her before she speaks: 'why feignest thou thyself to be another?' No disguise fools the God who sees the heart. We may hide our face from men, but never our true self from the Lord.

Ahijah is blind with age, but God tells him all, so he greets her before she speaks: 'why feignest thou thyself to be another?' No disguise fools the God who sees the heart. We may hide our face from men, but never our true self from the Lord.

Ahijah pronounces doom: Jeroboam, lifted up by God, made other gods and cast Him behind his back, so his whole house will be swept away like refuse. Privilege abused brings the heaviest judgment. To be raised by God and then forsake Him is to invite a terrible fall.

Ahijah pronounces doom: Jeroboam, lifted up by God, made other gods and cast Him behind his back, so his whole house will be swept away like refuse. Privilege abused brings the heaviest judgment. To be raised by God and then forsake Him is to invite a terrible fall.

The sick child will die the moment she enters the city — the only one of Jeroboam's house to be mourned, for in him alone was found some good. God's judgments are exact and even merciful; He takes the one tender soul before the storm falls on the rest.

The sick child will die the moment she enters the city — the only one of Jeroboam's house to be mourned, for in him alone was found some good. God's judgments are exact and even merciful; He takes the one tender soul before the storm falls on the rest.

The sick child will die the moment she enters the city — the only one of Jeroboam's house to be mourned, for in him alone was found some good. God's judgments are exact and even merciful; He takes the one tender soul before the storm falls on the rest.

Israel itself will be uprooted and scattered beyond the river, because Jeroboam sinned and made Israel to sin. One man's idolatry drags a whole nation toward ruin. The leader who teaches others to sin bears the guilt of all he led astray.

Israel itself will be uprooted and scattered beyond the river, because Jeroboam sinned and made Israel to sin. One man's idolatry drags a whole nation toward ruin. The leader who teaches others to sin bears the guilt of all he led astray.

Jeroboam's wife returns home, and as she steps over the threshold, the child dies, exactly as the prophet said. Not a word of God falls to the ground. His warnings are not threats to be waited out, but certainties that arrive on time.

Jeroboam's wife returns home, and as she steps over the threshold, the child dies, exactly as the prophet said. Not a word of God falls to the ground. His warnings are not threats to be waited out, but certainties that arrive on time.

Jeroboam reigns twenty-two years, then dies, and his son Nadab succeeds him — under the doom already pronounced. The dynasty built on golden calves is living on borrowed time. A house founded on sin cannot stand, however long judgment seems delayed.

Jeroboam reigns twenty-two years, then dies, and his son Nadab succeeds him — under the doom already pronounced. The dynasty built on golden calves is living on borrowed time. A house founded on sin cannot stand, however long judgment seems delayed.

Meanwhile Rehoboam reigns in Judah, and the land fills with high places, idols, and shameful practices, doing 'evil in the sight of the LORD.' The southern kingdom is no better than the north. Temple and city did not keep Judah from sliding into the sins of the nations.

Shishak of Egypt comes up and strips the temple and palace of their gold, and Rehoboam replaces the gold shields with bronze. The glory of Solomon's reign is already tarnishing. Sin drains away what God gave; the gold of obedience is exchanged for the bronze of decline.

Shishak of Egypt comes up and strips the temple and palace of their gold, and Rehoboam replaces the gold shields with bronze. The glory of Solomon's reign is already tarnishing. Sin drains away what God gave; the gold of obedience is exchanged for the bronze of decline.

Rehoboam dies after a reign marked by war with Jeroboam, and his son Abijam succeeds him. The split kingdom settles into rivalry and decay. So ends a generation that squandered the peace and glory it inherited — a sober warning to guard what God entrusts.
